metadata.split_role: Expanded porcini, Tylopilus, dark-pored, and brown bolete records.

records.culinary_uses: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation. Keep it for recognition/comparison until source review is cleared.

mushroom_profile.summary: Source-limited porcini-type entry for Almost Bluing King Bolete.
staining: Weak or limited blue bruising possible
research_notes: Still needs a strong species-level non-Wikipedia source for morphology and edible-use review before source debt can be cleared.
Consumer-app/site leads tie the names Bluing King Bolete, Blueing King Bolete, and Blue-staining porcini to Boletus subcaerulescens; kept here as search aliases only, not source-of-record support.
Food-route demoted in v4.3.175 until stronger species-level edible-use support is attached.

records.general_notes: Source/status cleanup v4.3.170: record remains visible but review-needed; current attached data does not yet clear species-level edible use.
records.non_edible_severity: Caution / source review; not recommended as food
records.edibility_detail: Do not eat from this app entry. Use it for recognition and comparison only until stronger species-level edible-use support is attached.
records.field_identification: Porcini-like bolete with whitish to yellow pores, firm white flesh, brown cap, and possible faint/limited blue staining. Compare against bitter Tylopilus and red/yellow boletes.
records.edibility_notes: A porcini-type edible by some accounts when confidently identified and in good condition, but this record is not source-cleared yet.

images_structured.author: Dave in NE PA

images_structured.rights_notes: Verified from Wikimedia Commons source page/license metadata. Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required; share-alike applies to adapted versions.
images_structured.rights_note: Verified from Wikimedia Commons source page/license metadata. Commercial use and modification allowed; attribution required; share-alike applies to adapted versions.

records.safety: Source review; do not eat from this app entry
records.food_value: Recognition only / not a food target in this guide
records.quality_notes: Potential porcini-type table value only when firm, young, non-buggy, correctly identified, and supported by stronger source review.
records.eating_use_notes: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation.
records.risk_reason: Source review remains unresolved for this uncommon porcini-type bolete; use this app entry for ID/caution only until strong species-level edible-use support is attached.
records.hard_rule: Do not eat from this app entry. Use it for recognition and comparison only until source review is cleared.
records.danger_level: Caution / source review; not recommended as food

lookalike_risk.label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.short_label: High — bolete confusion, severe-GI/bitter look-alikes possible
lookalike_risk.summary: Edible bolete use needs pore color, staining, host/substrate, taste where appropriate, and separation from false king, bitter Tylopilus, and red-pored/fast-blue-staining caution boletes.
required_checks: Reject bitter taste where safe nibble-and-spit is appropriate
lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not eat unidentified boletes; red/orange pores, bitter taste, or violent blue staining mean stop.

dangerous_lookalikes.warning: False king bolete is a northern/Great Lakes porcini-type danger concern.
target_traits: Pale netting near upper stem when expected
lookalike_traits: Hemlock or northern Great Lakes false-king concern
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If false king traits are possible, do not eat.

target_traits: White/yellow pores without bitter Tylopilus posture
required_checks: Tiny nibble-and-spit only if you already know the mushroom is safe to taste
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Bitter taste means not food; one specimen can ruin the pan.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not recommended red-pored blue-staining bolete group.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.

source_review_notes: Source-blocked edible/caution claim normalized to comparison/ID use in v4.3.175; food route should remain off until strong mushroom source support is attached.
records.mushroom_card_note: Source-limited porcini-type bolete; keep as ID/caution only until species-level edible-use support is cleared.

records.culinary_uses: Bitter; not a food target. One bitter bolete can wreck a meal.

mushroom_profile.summary: Bitter Tylopilus with pale cap skin that can peel or fragment; useful to recognize so it does not ruin a basket of boletes.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Summer and fall; northeastern/Midwestern distribution noted in keys.
research_notes: White to pale cap, brown-staining stem, bitter taste, and a thin papery cap surface that may peel or fragment with age. In the Tylopilus group, taste is a spit-out clue only.

records.edibility_detail: Do not put bitter or uncertain Tylopilus/Retiboletus-style boletes in a meal; one bitter specimen can wreck the whole dish.
records.mushroom_card_note: Bitter Tylopilus with pale cap skin that can peel or fragment; useful to recognize so it does not ruin a basket of boletes.
records.field_identification: White to pale cap, brown-staining stem, bitter taste, and a thin papery cap surface that may peel or fragment with age. In the Tylopilus group, taste is a spit-out clue only.
records.risk_reason: The main issue is bitterness and bitter-bolete confusion; it is included so a bitter Tylopilus does not ruin a meal.
records.quality_notes: Included for recognition, comparison, or caution rather than table value.
records.eating_use_notes: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation.
records.hard_rule: Do not put bitter or uncertain Tylopilus/Retiboletus-style boletes in a meal; one bitter specimen can wreck the whole dish.
records.image_rights_audit_note: v4.3.160 cleared three unresolved Commons images; record remains visible as review-needed ID/comparison pending verified image recovery.

records.culinary_uses: Often treated as a good edible bolete with a mild taste, but use only with confident ID and watch for bitter Tylopilus look-alikes.
records.notes: Black Velvet Bolete: dark velvety black-brown cap and stem, white pores turning pinkish, and flesh/pore bruising red then black.

mushroom_profile.summary: Black Velvet Bolete: dark velvety black-brown cap and stem, white pores turning pinkish, and flesh/pore bruising red then black. Commons-backed images added.
research_notes: Dark blackish velvety cap, dark stem, white pore surface becoming pinkish, and bruising that turns red then brown/black are the key field marks. Often under oaks.
Look for the blackish velvety cap, dark smooth stem, white pore surface that matures pinkish, and red-to-black bruising. Often with oaks; older guides list it as Tylopilus alboater.

records.edibility_detail: Use only after confirming dark velvety cap/stem, pore-color changes, and red-to-black bruising pattern.
records.mushroom_card_note: Black Velvet Bolete: dark velvety black-brown cap and stem, white pores turning pinkish, and flesh/pore bruising red then black. Commons-backed images added.
records.field_identification: Look for the blackish velvety cap, dark smooth stem, white pore surface that matures pinkish, and red-to-black bruising. Often with oaks; older guides list it as Tylopilus alboater.

records.quality_notes: Dark Tylopilus-like bolete with fair-to-good food value only after advanced confirmation.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook confirmed specimens; do not use as a casual brown/dark bolete.
records.risk_reason: Risk is advanced ID: dark boletes and Tylopilus-like species can be confusing and some are poor food or not food targets.
records.hard_rule: Use only after confirming dark velvety cap/stem, pore-color changes, and red-to-black bruising pattern.

edible_use.notes: Cook confirmed specimens; do not use as a casual brown/dark bolete.

lookalike_risk.hard_rule: Do not eat unidentified boletes; red/orange pores, bitter taste, or violent blue staining mean stop.

lookalike_traits: Hemlock or northern Great Lakes false-king concern
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: If false king traits are possible, do not eat.

required_checks: Tiny nibble-and-spit only if you already know the mushroom is safe to taste
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Bitter taste means not food; one specimen can ruin the pan.

dangerous_lookalikes.risk: Poisonous / not recommended red-pored blue-staining bolete group.
dangerous_lookalikes.hard_rule: Red/orange pores or fast strong blue staining mean stop unless expert-confirmed.

records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds the source-backed macro spore-print description and normalized color filters; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.

records.culinary_uses: Edible reputation exists, but this guide treats it as a cautious ID-first species because blue-staining boletes need care.

mushroom_profile.summary: Straw-colored brittle bolete with dramatic blue staining and a yellow spore print; usually in sandy hardwood-edge habitats.
research_notes: Straw-yellow cap and stem, brittle/chalky texture, strong blue staining when cut or bruised, and yellow spore print separate it from most boletes.

records.edibility_detail: Do not treat every blue-staining bolete as this species; confirm straw-yellow color, brittle or hollowing stem, immediate blue staining, sandy/disturbed habitat, and yellow spore print.
records.mushroom_card_note: Straw-colored brittle bolete with dramatic blue staining and a yellow spore print; usually in sandy hardwood-edge habitats.
records.field_identification: Straw-yellow cap and stem, brittle/chalky texture, strong blue staining when cut or bruised, and yellow spore print separate it from most boletes.
records.food_value: Not a food target in this guide
records.risk_reason: Blue staining makes beginners overconfident; this record should teach the straw-yellow, brittle/hollowing, immediately blue-staining concept rather than invite casual eating.
records.quality_notes: Included for recognition, comparison, or caution rather than table value.
records.eating_use_notes: Do not use this app entry as a food recommendation.
records.hard_rule: Do not treat every blue-staining bolete as this species; confirm straw-yellow color, brittle or hollowing stem, immediate blue staining, sandy/disturbed habitat, and yellow spore print.
records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds the source-backed macro spore-print description and normalized color filters; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.

records.culinary_uses: Potentially good porcini-type edible with confident ID, but the North American edulis group is messy and should be handled conservatively.

mushroom_profile.summary: Eastern porcini-group bolete with wrinkled grayish-brown cap, hardwood association, white flesh, and a magenta/purple ammonia reaction on the cap.
pore_color: White pores becoming yellow/olive; not bruising

records.edibility_detail: Edible with expert-level porcini-group confirmation; do not use as a generic brown bolete.
records.mushroom_card_note: Eastern porcini-group bolete with wrinkled grayish-brown cap, hardwood association, white flesh, and a magenta/purple ammonia reaction on the cap.
records.field_identification: Porcini-type build, wrinkled or pocked grayish-brown cap, white pores becoming yellow/olive, prominent stem reticulation, white non-staining flesh, and hardwood association. Chemical reaction can help separate it from nearby edulis-like species.

use_links.label: Bolete Filter / Western Pennsylvania Mushroom Club: Boletus atkinsonii
use_links.notes: Supports Michigan presence, oak/beech morphology, confusion with Boletus variipes-fagicola, chemical test notes, and edible-use as Choice.

edible_use.notes: Cook only confidently identified young, firm specimens; reject bitter, old, buggy, waterlogged, red-pored, or strongly blue-staining lookalikes.

records.eating_use_notes: Cook only confirmed young, firm specimens; reject bitter, old, buggy, waterlogged, red-pored, or strongly blue-staining lookalikes.
records.quality_notes: Potentially useful porcini-group bolete when oak/beech habitat, field traits, and lookalike checks all fit; keep expert-ID framing.
records.risk_reason: Risk is confusing porcini-group boletes with bitter, red-pored, strongly blue-staining, or otherwise unsafe boletes.
records.hard_rule: Do not use as a generic brown bolete; confirm the full porcini-group field-trait set before considering food use.
records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds the source-backed macro spore-print description and normalized color filters; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.

records.culinary_uses: Porcini-group edible when confidently identified and in good condition.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Boletus Variipes-Fagicola.
mushroom_profile.season_note: Late summer and fall under oaks and other eastern hardwoods.
research_notes: v4.3.178: edible-use source support added from Bolete Filter / Western Pennsylvania Mushroom Club; keep expert-ID and condition cautions.
pore_color: White young becoming yellowish to olive

records.edibility_detail: Use the visible canonical record only when reticulate-stem and porcini-type traits are confirmed.
records.field_identification: Dry tan to grayish-brown cap, white young pore surface becoming yellowish or olive, non-bruising flesh and pores, and a usually prominent whitish reticulation over a brownish stem under eastern hardwoods.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus variipes
use_links.notes: Oak/hardwood association, non-bruising pores, and reticulate stem.

use_links.label: Bolete Filter / Western Pennsylvania Mushroom Club: Boletus variipes
use_links.notes: Supports Michigan presence, reticulate-stem porcini traits, var. fagicola as a darker cap/stem form, and edible-use as Choice.

records.edibility_notes: Porcini-group edible when confidently identified and in good condition.

edible_use.notes: Cook confirmed young, firm specimens; reject bitter, old, buggy, or waterlogged specimens.

records.quality_notes: Porcini ally with edible-use source support when reticulate stem, pale-to-yellow/olive pores, non-staining white flesh, and oak/hardwood habitat all fit.
records.eating_use_notes: Cook confirmed young, firm specimens; reject bitter, old, buggy, or waterlogged specimens.
records.risk_reason: Risk is over-broad use of variable-stalk/porcini-like names without confirming reticulation, pores, flesh, taste, and host habitat.
records.hard_rule: Use the visible canonical record only when reticulate-stem and porcini-type traits are confirmed.

records.culinary_uses: Not treated as a food target; included because it is distinctive and taxonomically odd.

mushroom_profile.summary: Odd oak-associated bolete with soft brown cap, dull yellow boletinoid pores running slightly down the stem, and brown-to-reddish bruising.
research_notes: Soft brown cap, dull yellow pore surface that begins to run down the stem, slow brown to reddish-brown bruising, and brown reticulation at the stem apex. Usually associated with oaks.

records.mushroom_card_note: Odd oak-associated bolete with soft brown cap, dull yellow boletinoid pores running slightly down the stem, and brown-to-reddish bruising.
records.field_identification: Soft brown cap, dull yellow pore surface that begins to run down the stem, slow brown to reddish-brown bruising, and brown reticulation at the stem apex. Usually associated with oaks.
records.source_note: v4.3.210 adds the source-backed macro spore-print description and normalized color filters; spore color alone is not proof of species identity or edibility.

records.culinary_uses: Reported edible when confidently identified, but keep as an experienced-forager bolete in this guide.

mushroom_profile.summary: Minimal bolete seed entry for Butter Foot Bolete.
staining: Usually not bruising blue; may deepen yellow
mushroom_profile.spore_print: Yellow-brown, sometimes with an olive tinge.
research_notes: v4.3.178: edible-use source support added from Bolete Filter / Western Pennsylvania Mushroom Club; keep expert-ID and condition cautions.

records.edibility_detail: Confirm the yellow stem, yellow pores, oak/hardwood habitat, fine stem reticulation, and lack of blue bruising before considering food use.
records.field_identification: Brown to yellow-brown cap, bright yellow stem, yellow pore surface becoming olive-yellow, usually fine reticulation on the stem, and little to no blue staining in hardwood/oak forests.

use_links.label: MushroomExpert: Boletus auripes
use_links.notes: Yellow stem, oak association, non-bruising yellow pores, and reticulation.

use_links.label: Bolete Filter / Western Pennsylvania Mushroom Club: Boletus auripes
use_links.notes: Supports Michigan presence, yellow stem and yellow pore traits, non-staining behavior, chemical test notes, and edible-use as Choice.

records.edibility_notes: Reported edible when confidently identified, but keep as an experienced-forager bolete in this guide. Confirm the yellow stem, yellow pores, oak/hardwood habitat, fine stem reticulation, and lack of blue bruising before considering food use.
